sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Jacqueline, presents with anxiety regarding recent blood work showing hepatitis C, which was not previously known to her.
She reports feeling fine despite this diagnosis.
She has a history of IV drug use in the past, but has been clean for over 15 years.
She also reports a history of excessive alcohol use, currently consuming one beer per day, and occasional increased consumption on weekends.
She smokes 1-2 cigarettes per day, having decreased from a pack and a half.
She denies any other medical conditions besides hypertension, diabetes, and depression, which run in her family.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of IV drug use, excessive alcohol use, and smoking.
She has a family history of hypertension, diabetes, and depression.
Review of systems: The patient reports feeling tired at the end of the day despite getting enough sleep.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient appears in no distress and no jaundice on skin exam.
The heart exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm with a 2/6 systolic murmur, which has not changed from last year.
The lungs are clear with no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
The abdomen is soft with no hepatosplenomegaly.
The musculoskeletal exam is normal.
Test Results: The patient's HCV antibody test was positive, and her liver panel showed an elevated AST at 39, with normal albumin and total bilirubin levels.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a confirmed diagnosis of hepatitis C, likely acquired years ago, with initial labs consistent with this diagnosis.
Problem list: Hepatitis C; Hypertension; Diabetes; Depression.
Plan:
I ordered additional blood work to confirm the diagnosis, including checking the HCV RNA and HCV genotype.
I also ordered an ultrasound to determine the severity of liver disease, specifically fibrosis.
I will use this information to determine the next steps in treatment.
I advised the patient to have her family members screened for HCV.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in three weeks to review the results of the additional testing and determine the next steps in treatment.
